string-array.name: level_strings
string-array.item:
"Utilise l'éditeur de génome pour créer une espèce qui prospère dans l'environnement de ce défi.
Quand tu es prêt, mets une cellule sur le substrat sous le microscope et décongèle-le pour voir s'il est viable. Il faut 100 cellules pour compléter ce défi."

"La cellule par défaut sera suffisante pour ce défi. Toutes cellules ont besoin d'énergie, et les Photocytes disponibles dans ce défi obtiennent leur énergie de la lumière du Soleil.
La lumière du Soleil impacte la partie éclairée en haut du substrat."
Appuie sur la partie éclairée en haut de la boite de Pétri pour ajouter une cellule par défaut et modifie la température pour observer ce qu'il se passe.

"Cette fois-ci l'objectif est de créer une espèce qui peut se multiplier jusqu'à au moins 150 cellules sur le même substrat que le défi précédent.
Ce défi et beaucoup d'autres ne sont pas requis pour continuer dans le jeu. Vous pouvez le résoudre plus tard si vous le trouvez difficile pour l'instant."
Les cellules petites ont tendance à être en plus grand nombres. La propriété Masse de séparation pourrait être utile.
Ce substrat a une viscosité bien plus basse que les précédents. Pouvez-vous concevoir un espèce qui survie dans cet environnement?
Une propriété utile dans l'éditeur de génome est Faire de l'adhésine.

"Un autre type de cellule est désormais disponible, le Phagocyte. Il peut manger les petits points marrons que vous avez peut-être vu apparaitre quand les cellules meurent. Manger ces petits nutriments les fait grandir.
Aucune source de lumière n'est présente dans ce défi, mais des bouts de nutriments sont constamment saupoudrés sur le substrat. Pouvez-vous créer une espèce qui se multiplie jusqu'à 300 cellules?"
Placez simplement quelques Phagocytes avec des paramètres par défaut sur le substrat et observez patiemment. Dans un peu de temps vous devriez avoir 300 cellules.

"Pouvez-vous obtenir 650 cellules sur le même substrat qu'avant?
Vous avez désormais accès au Flagellocytes dans le labo expérimental, mais dans ce défi nous travaillons encore avec les mêmes contraintes que le défi précédent pour voir si nous pouvons optimiser notre organisme sans les Flagellocytes.
You can always skip this challenge and go directly to challenge 9 where you can use Flagellocytes if you like."
"The properties \"Split angle\" and \"Split mass\" might be useful to solve this challenge. The cells spread more efficiently if they don't end up on a line."

"In this challenge you have 100 h to get 5 red and 5 yellow cells. Only problem, you can not place any cells. You do, however, have access to the cell boost tool. This tool lets you make cells grow and split.
Can you figure out which cells to boost to get the correct number of cells of each color?"

"We have found a beautiful, and tasty, red cell in nature. We would like to have more of it to improve the institute canteen but since it has a strict no-GMO policy, we cannot recreate this by copying it to the genome editor. Can you make more of this cell using some traditional breeding strategy?"
Use the move tool to help this cell
Can you get over 400 cells during 50 h on this substrate by just adding one initial cell?
You will need your initial cell to divide into two cells with different functions.

"We are going to try to use your new cell, the Flagellocyte, for this challenge. This cell can not gain energy from the environment, but it can swim! Note that this cell immediately dies if attached to another cell such that the flagellum is obstructed.
The viscosity of this substrate is higher than in the previous \"Macrophages\" challenge. Can you nevertheless make a species multiply to 420 cells?"

"To solve this challenge you will need your organism's life cycle to include three different modes.
Ticking \"Contaminate with random cells\" in experimental mode and running for a little while might give you some ideas on how to solve this challenge."

"Have mode 1 split to mode 2 and 3. Have mode 3 split to 3 and 1. Let mode 1 and 3 be Phagocytes and mode 2 Flagellocyte. Tick \"Make Adhesin\" for mode 1.
Note that this is one among many different ways to solve this challenge."

"The environment of this challenge is less friendly than the previous one. Can you nevertheless get 300 cells?
This requires some serious optimization of your cells. Note that all challenges do not have to be solved to progress in the game."

"Having trouble solving some of the challenges and need some inspiration? Let evolution do the job for you! Go to the \"Challenges\" tab of the main menu and make a long press on this challenge to open it in experimental mode. Put the solution you found for the Macrophages III challenge on the substrate (you can save and load genomes in the genome editor).
Now start the simulation and you should have some cells multiplying in the environment. If you now increase the radiation level from 0 to slightly above 0 the cells will start mutating and soon you will have more cells than you started with since they have adapted to their environment. Once they reach the required limit you can copy a cell to the genome editor, save it, and use it in this challenge."
The light source is now moving around the substrate, much like the sun. Your goal is to design cells that can multiply up to 130 cells with this rotating light source.
You will need to make a cell that can swim around the edge of the substrate, following the light. Maybe you can modify your solution of challenge \"Macrophages III\" slightly?
The only source of energy on this substrate is the sun. Can you nevertheless create a viable species without using photocytes?
The present photocytes leave small pieces of nutrients behind when they die. Can you make a species that lives off of them?

"A new type of cell is now available, the Lipocyte. This cell contains fat and can thus store more energy than other cells. These cells also consume very little energy.
This substrate contains no energy source, can you nevertheless keep 16 live cells on it for 50 h?"
Use a Lipocyte with very low split mass and just wait.
The light source is now moving faster. Your goal is to design cells that can multiply up to 500 cells with this itinerant light source.
Your cells can never swim fast enough to keep up with the sun. Instead, accumulate energy when it is abundant for use during dark times.

"Make a long worm consisting of both Photocytes and Lipocytes. One way is to have:
Mode 1: Photocyte, split to 2,3 Mode 2: Lipocyte, will not split Mode 3: Photocyte, split to 1,1
Make adhesin for all of them."
Cells now die when they reach the edge of the substrate. Can you make a species maintaining a cell count above 150 for 100 h?

"Having trouble solving some of the challenges and need some inspiration? Let evolution do the job for you! Go to the \"Challenges\" tab of the main menu and make a long press on this challenge to open it in experimental mode. Put the solution you found for the Macrophages III challenge on the substrate (you can save and load genomes in the genome editor).
Now start the simulation and you should have some cells multiplying in the environment. If you now increase the radiation level from 0 to slightly above 0 the cells will start mutating and soon you will have more cells than you started with since they have adapted to their environment. Once they reach the required limit you can copy a cell to the genome editor, save it, and use it in this challenge."

Your research team has developed a new type of cell that you need to test. The Devorocyte sucks nutrients from other cells upon touching them. Can you decrease the number of cells in this substrate to below 300?
Our micropipette is under maintenance so we can not insert new cells at the moment. Can you anyways get 100 red cells on this Petri dish?
There is some mutagen present on this substrate, use it to your advantage.
You forgot a sample in the incubator for a week and some new species developed. They spread spores very efficiently and infested all your samples. Can you devise a method to remove them?
Spend more time using the microscope than engineering a genome.

"Your collaborators have been able to develop a cell immune to attacks from your Devorocytes. The Keratinocyte can defend itself and cells attached directly to it from hostile cells.
Can you help them design an organism able to disrupt the ecosystem living on this plate? They want at least 420 cells."
Make a swimming organism consisting of three cells, a Keratinocyte in the center, a Flagellocyte in the rear, and a Phagocyte in the front.

"Let mode 1, 3 and 5 be Phagocytes, mode 2 Flagellocyte, and finally mode 4 a Keratinocyte. Tick \"Make Adhesin\" for mode 1 and 3.
Have mode 1 split to mode 2 and 3 (in that order). Now we have an ordinary swimmer. Have mode 3 split to 4 and 5. Now we have an immune swimmer. Have mode 5 split to 5 and 1. Now we release eggs for new swimmers.
Note that this is one among many different ways to solve this challenge."

"Remove all the red cells! You need your cells to survive at least 30 h after the red cells died to make sure your cells can survive on their own.
This challenge must be solved within 220 h."

A new cell is available. The Buoyocyte has a compartment that can contain a liquid of a different density than other cells. See if you can use it to create life on this substrate which has been oriented vertically so that cells are slowly sinking to the bottom of it. 350 cells should indicate that they can live and reproduce.
You will need to lower the density setting of your Buoyocytes.

Now the substrate is still oriented vertically but the material of the substrate is of a density close to the density of the cells so they do not fall. Can you make a species that multiplies to 400 cell without using Flagellocytes?

"The density of this slippery substrate varies with height, making cells collect in the center.
Some species is already living here but can you get another one living here for at least 500h without using phagocytes?"
You need to kill all cells on this substrate but lost your bottle of sterilizer, you have to think of something else.
Can you make an organism quickly take over this crowded substrate?
In this sample, viscosity is nonexistent. Can you make an organism capable of surviving and thriving in these harsh conditions?
Try to make your organism stick to the lit area using the Glueocyte.
Some kind of worm-like organism has attached itself to the top of our substrate. We need to get it rid of it so we can use the Petri dish for our next experiment.

"We here have a moss sperm and a moss egg that we want to fertilize for our moss breeding program.
The sperm is able to avoid black cells but not the red ones. Can you help it reach the egg?"
Can you figure out what substrate this is and get 450 cells to live on it?

"In this sample there are no nitrates in the substrate. Though a new cell which can bind nitrogen, the Nitrocyte, is now available. This cell can supply connected cells with nitrates. Any cell can store a rather large amount of nitrates so they need not be connected to this cell. Cells do not consume the nitrates, though they are shared between the daughter cells when a cell divides. If a cell does not have enough nitrates to divide into two cells, it simply will not divide.
Can you make an organism able to multiply into 200 cells in this environment?"
No sun this time. Can you get 400 cells?

"Most plants in the legume family live in symbiosis with nitrogen fixating bacteria called rhizobia living in their root systems. The bacteria provides nitrogen to the plants while the plants provide nutrients to the bacteria.
On this substrate we have a couple of very tiny plants of the legume family. Unfortunately their rhizobia was killed by a virus and the plants as a result have stopped growing due to lack of nitrogen.
Can you design some new organisms to take the role of the rhizobia?"
Glueocytes can help transfer nutrients between different species.
Can you get 700 cells on this peculiar substrate?

"The Virocyte is a cell infected with a virus. If another cell touches this cell it risks also getting infected unless it is protected by a Keratinocyte. Part of this cells genome will get copied into the other cell when it gets infected. This is very much how a virus works in nature.
Which part of the genome gets copied is specified by the \"Virus copy from\" property in the genome editor. Note that not only the genes for one mode are copied but also the genes for the modes of 2 generations of descendants. So a maximum of 1+2+4 modes of the host genome are copied to the newly infected cell.
In this challenge you are supposed to kill all the initial cells by infecting them with a virus and leave the substrate clean."
Tap on a cell under the microscope and press \"Load\" in the Genome editor and select \"Load from microscope\" to inspect the organisms living on this substrate before designing a virus.

"As part of our labs public outreach initiative we are attempting to set the world record for the smallest maze.
Can you test it to make sure it works?"
Viruses are often specifically evolved to infect a certain host. Can you make a virus infecting these organisms leaving the substrate clean?
Tap on a cell under the microscope and press \"Load\" in the Genome editor and select \"Load from microscope\" to inspect the organisms living on this substrate before designing a virus.

"The order of infection is important when different viruses overwrites the same part of a cells genome.
These viruses have been designed to create an interesting organism by infecting a simple lipocyte. Their order has been messed up, can you figure out the correct order of infection?"
In the main settings of cell lab there is an option to show cell types next to mode names. Turning that on is useful to inspect the genomes of these viruses.
The objective is once again to remove all the cells from the substrate, which now does not contain any nitrates.

"Gene therapy is a method practiced in medicine to correct a defective genome by inserting new genes. The new genes are typically inserted using a virus as a vector. A special virus is prepared that does not cause much harm but inserts a missing gene.
On this substrate we have some particularly ugly cells, specifically, mother nature did a very bad job of matching the color of the Phagocyte to the color of the Flagellocyte. We don't have access to the Flagellocyte genes but would like some more visually appealing swimmers than these. Could you do some gene therapy on these cells to obtain about the same number of swimmers but in a color scheme of your liking instead? They need to survive more than 200 h."

"You've run out of the ordinary nutrients for your cells. In desperation to feed them you saved a piece of bacon from lunch but now realize your phagocytes can not feed on fat.
Luckily, the researchers in the molecular biology lab have already been working on a similar scenario and were able to develop Secrocytes that produce Lipase, an enzyme that can break down fat into fatty acids that the phagocytes can eat."

"An engineered organisms used to make biofuel from sunlight is living on this Petri dish.
Can you make an organism living off of the lipids that are released from the dying lipocytes and thus ruining our production?"

"This diverse ecosystem reproduces sexually using a new cell type, the gamete. This cell doesn't have a complete set of chromosomes and can thus not split.
However, it can fuse with another compatible gamete to form a new cell with complete genome. After fusing the mode is changed and the lifecycle can continue. This way beneficial traits can efficiently spread through a population.
The ecosystem on this dish unfortunately went through a population bottleneck when professor Challenger spilled a mug of coffee on it. Now the gene for swimming fast is missing. Can you reintroduce it to the gene pool?"

"You have been experimenting with a swimming Photocyte organism. Unfortunately, your light system started malfunctioning and is now rotating slowly.
Your organism is not fit enough to keep up. You have to introduce it to a new diet in order to keep it alive!"

"This ancient green organism was found living in an acidic lake. Archaeologists have in the same lake found microscopic fossils of a—now extinct—predator species thought to once live off of these green creatures. To corroborate this theory we would like to see if there are some traces of their interaction left in the genome of the green species. To test our theory we have placed a synthetic predator species on this substrate to see if some protective mechanism can be awoken from the depths of the green swimmers genome. That didn't happen.
Upon inspection of the genome we have found that a recent mutation has taken a shortcut, the M3 cells no longer split to M5,M6, but it splits to M3,M4. Perhaps this mutation proliferated as a result of the extinction of the predator? Anyhow, can you—without inserting a keratinocyte—restore the old behavior and see what the species does in this synthetic environment?
If you get 400 cells without actually introducing a keratinocyte then we have confirmed that this species has genes to protect itself from the predator and thus the fossil predators were most likely once living off of this organism."
Three species are living on this substrate. Can you make them all die?
Two species are living on this substrate. Can you add a third?

"Our politicians have introduced patent laws to help foster technological advancements.
We can now not use the solution to Floaters II anymore without paying a huge license fee for the Buoyocytes. Our budget now only allows for one Buoyocyte per genome and we can thus not make an equally efficient species anymore.
We have had to lower our ambitions for this substrate to 200 cells. Can you work around the limitations posed by the patent laws by using the Neurocyte?"
Use the longest time on the oscillator preset. Make sure to set the Neurocyte adhesin connections to what the case is in your organism.
This substrate has dried up a bit and swimming is not very efficient any more. Can you use the recently developed Myocyte to find another means of locomotion?
The muscle contract and lift properties are useful here. Perhaps assign them to different channels.
Make a three-celled linear organism with a Myocyte in the center. Setting the split ratio and mass in a good way is crucial.

"Our field biologists have found a tiny organism that exhibits a defence behaviour when subject to a certain stimulus.
Can you figure out what the stimulus is?"
This substrate is even drier, and the cell limits are higher. Can you still make it?
Making a slightly easier version of this substrate and using evolution can help you solve this. Let it run for a night :)

"We now have access to two new cells, Senseocyte and the Sterocyte. They can sense smell from either food or cells of certain colors. The Senseocyte will output a signal substance based on the smell molecule concentration. The Stereocyte will output a signal substance based on the left-right concentration gradient of smell molecules.
Food is now very scarce, can you figure out a way to survive on this substrate?"
Use the Stereocyte and Myocyte for steering towards food. Think about the placement of the cells within your organism.

"Make a simple swimmer so that once it gets enough food it will split into a four-celled linear organism, Flagellocyte-Myocyte-Stereocyte-Phagocyte. Make the Myocyte bend according to the smell direction picked up by the Stereocyte.
Tuning the Myocyte response is crucial and requires some experimenting.
Let the Phagocyte split off the simple swimmers that will sometimes be lucky and develop into these four-celled navigating swimmers and thus letting your organism reproduce.
Setting the adhesin strength high and tuning the split masses is crucial
Note that this is one of many ways of solving this challenge."
Can you make a species survive here without using the Myocyte?

"This Petri dish is slippery and has no rim so cells fall and die when they reach the edge.
Can you nevertheless get 300 cells?"
Can you optimize an organism to get 600 cells on this substrate?
This challenge is similar to Digestion, but here food is more scarce.
Can you make a predator reproduce to 50 cells and lower the prey population to 300 cells?

"Now we have access to the Secrocyte. This cell can secrete different molecules to its surrounding.
Can you make a parasitic organism live off of these harvesting organisms?"
The Glueocyte is useful here. Giving your organism some mobility might be helpful.
"The red predator doesn't have any food nearby. Can you deliver it to it?"

"This substrate has high salinity and very low viscosity with huge nutrient chunks. A paradise for smart swimmers. Alas, we cannot use Flagellocytes.
Can you still make an organism thriving in this environment?"
A technique we call mitotic propulsion is useful here.
Some mine-laying organism is living on this plate. Can you give them a taste of their own medicine and leave the plate clean afterwards?
"On this plate we have two similar species. Can you eradicate the red one while keeping the blue one alive? Don't leave any of your own cells on the plate when you are done."
Can you make a species reproduce to 150 cells while keeping the initial cell count low for 100 h?
Can you make a species reproduce to 200 cells while keeping the initial cell count low for 200 h? Now the initial cells are moving.
"We have a skin sample from a patient with skin cancer. Can you engineer an organism that can remove the cancer cells but keep the rest of the skin intact?"
This substrate is all dried up and also has almost no food. Can you get 200 cells?
